title: Juniper Networks Junos OS Evolved PTX Series Critical Pre-Auth RCE (CVE-2026-21902, CVSS 9.8): Unauthenticated Attacker Gains Root Access via Exposed Anomaly Detection REST API on Port 8160 — Network Infrastructure at Risk, Emergency Patches Released
affected_products: Juniper Networks Junos OS Evolved 25.4.x (prior to 25.4R1-S1-EVO)
Juniper Networks Junos OS Evolved 25.4.x (prior to 25.4R2-EVO)

sources.name: watchTowr Labs - Sometimes, You Can Just Feel The Security In The Design
BleepingComputer - Critical Juniper Networks PTX flaw allows full router takeover
Juniper Networks Security Portal - 2026-02 Out-of-Cycle Security Bulletin
Rescana - Critical CVE-2026-21902 Vulnerability in Juniper Networks PTX Series Routers
SecurityWeek - Juniper Networks PTX Routers Affected by Critical Vulnerability
UpGuard - Critical Juniper Networks RCE CVE-2026-21902
Purple Ops - CVE-2026-21902 Junos Evolved RCE (CVSS 9.8)
RunZero - Juniper Networks Junos OS Evolved: CVE-2026-21902
Threat Intelligence Report - Critical Juniper PTX flaw enables unauthenticated root takeover
TheHackerWire - Junos OS Evolved PTX: Critical RCE in Anomaly Detection Framework

timeline.event: Juniper Networks discovered the vulnerability internally during product security testing
Juniper Networks issued 2026-02 Out-of-Cycle Security Bulletin; watchTowr Labs published detailed research analysis
Patches released: Junos OS Evolved 25.4R1-S1-EVO, 25.4R2-EVO, and 26.2R1-EVO
Major security news outlets (BleepingComputer, SecurityWeek) begin coverage
No evidence of active in-the-wild exploitation reported at time of latest advisories
Threat remains active; unpatched organizations continue to be at risk, 40 days post-disclosure

simple_summary.what_happened: A critical vulnerability exists in Juniper Networks PTX Series routers running Junos OS Evolved. The On-Box Anomaly Detection framework — which should only be accessible internally — is incorrectly exposed on port 8160/TCP without any authentication, allowing remote attackers to execute commands as root without logging in.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations using Juniper PTX Series routers running Junos OS Evolved versions 25.4.x prior to patches 25.4R1-S1-EVO, 25.4R2-EVO, or 26.2R1-EVO. This includes service providers, data centers, and enterprises using Juniper's high-performance routing platforms for core network infrastructure.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Network attacker scans for Juniper PTX routers with port 8160/TCP accessible from their network position. Step 2: Attacker identifies vulnerable Junos OS Evolved 25.4 versions running the default anomaly detection framework. Step 3: Attacker sends crafted HTTP requests to port 8160/TCP targeting the Python REST API — no credentials required. Step 4: Attacker gains root access to the router and executes arbitrary operating system commands. Step 5: Attacker maintains persistent access, intercepts traffic, manipulates routing tables and protocols, or pivots to other network infrastructure.
what_you_should_do: Immediately patch affected PTX routers to Junos OS Evolved 25.4R1-S1-EVO, 25.4R2-EVO, or 26.2R1-EVO
If immediate patching is not feasible, block access to port 8160/TCP using firewall rules or router ACLs, restricting to trusted management networks only
Disable the anomaly detection framework as an emergency measure using: request pfe anomalies disable
Audit network logs for HTTP/HTTPS requests to port 8160 on PTX routers for signs of prior exploitation
Verify router integrity and check for unauthorized configuration changes or added admin accounts
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: This vulnerability enables complete device takeover without authentication from any network-accessible attacker. PTX routers are core network infrastructure used by service providers and large enterprises. A compromise allows traffic interception, data exfiltration, routing protocol manipulation, and lateral movement across critical infrastructure networks. The default-enabled nature of the vulnerable service and its CVSS 9.8 score make this an extremely high-value target for nation-state actors and cybercriminals targeting network infrastructure.

tactics.description: Attacker exploits the unauthenticated REST API exposed on port 8160 — no credentials or user interaction required, only network access to the router management interface
Attacker executes arbitrary code as root through the anomaly detection framework's workflow scheduling and command execution capabilities
The vulnerability directly grants root privileges, escalating from unauthenticated network access to full OS-level control
Attacker modifies router configuration and persistent state to maintain access and evade detection by network monitoring
Attacker intercepts and captures network traffic flowing through the compromised core router, gaining access to sensitive organizational communications

detection_guidance.summary: Detection should focus on identifying attempted exploitation of the unauthenticated REST API on port 8160/TCP and unusual process execution from the anomaly detection framework running as root. Network-based detection should monitor for HTTP/HTTPS traffic to port 8160 on PTX routers. Host-based detection should monitor for unexpected process spawning and configuration changes.

log_sources.source: Juniper PTX Router Syslog and Event Logs
log_sources.description: Router system logs capturing connection attempts to port 8160, authentication failures, and anomalous process execution from the anomaly detection framework
Network-based intrusion detection systems monitoring for HTTP/HTTPS requests to port 8160 on PTX routers or unusual REST API call patterns
Network flow data identifying traffic patterns to port 8160/TCP from external or suspicious sources

log_sources.source: Router Management Platform Logs (Junos Space, NMS)
log_sources.description: Centralized network management system logs capturing unexpected configuration changes or API activity on PTX routers
Cross-correlate router events with authentication logs to detect unauthorized access attempts and successful exploitation

what_to_look_for.description: Any inbound traffic to TCP port 8160 on PTX routers should be investigated, as this port should only be accessible from internal management networks

what_to_look_for.indicator: REST API calls without authentication headers
what_to_look_for.description: HTTP POST/PUT requests to the anomaly detection REST API endpoints lacking valid authentication tokens or credentials
what_to_look_for.log_field: dest_port=8160 AND http_method IN (POST, PUT) AND auth_header=''

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unexpected process spawning from anomaly detection service
what_to_look_for.description: Child processes spawned from the PFE anomaly detection daemon running as root — indicates active exploitation
Router configuration modifications originating from network sources other than designated management stations

what_to_look_for.indicator: Persistent connections to port 8160 from external IPs
what_to_look_for.description: Long-lived or repeated connections to port 8160 from external IP ranges, indicating active exploitation or reconnaissance
what_to_look_for.log_field: dest_port=8160 AND src_ip NOT IN (trusted_mgmt_ranges) AND connection_duration > 60

immediate_actions.description: Immediately audit your network inventory for all Juniper PTX Series routers running Junos OS Evolved 25.4.x to determine which devices require patching. Use network management tools to query Junos version across your fleet.
immediate_actions.command: show version (on affected PTX routers via management console)

immediate_actions.description: Download and apply patches Junos OS Evolved 25.4R1-S1-EVO, 25.4R2-EVO, or 26.2R1-EVO from Juniper Networks support portal. Plan a maintenance window for router updates to minimize service disruption.
If immediate patching is not possible, deploy firewall rules or router ACLs to drop all inbound traffic to TCP port 8160 unless originating from trusted management networks.
immediate_actions.command: set firewall filter BLOCK_8160 term deny-8160 from destination-port 8160 then discard

immediate_actions.description: As an alternative emergency mitigation, disable the vulnerable anomaly detection service on affected routers to eliminate the attack surface until patching can be completed.

Search all available network and system logs from February 20, 2026 to present for signs of exploitation attempts against port 8160 on PTX routers. Check for unauthorized configuration changes or new admin accounts.
